children.name: Anhinga
children.description: The sole genus of the family, containing four species of slender, snake-necked waterbirds found across warm freshwaters worldwide. Unlike most waterbirds, darters lack waterproof feathers and must spread their wings to dry after each fishing foray, a familiar sight along tropical waterways.

children.name: Anhinga anhinga
children.description: The only New World darter, commonly called the 'snakebird' for its habit of swimming with only its slender neck and dagger-like bill above the waterline. Males are glossy black with silvery wing patches, while females have a pale tan head and neck.

children.name: Anhinga melanogaster
children.description: Found across South and Southeast Asia from India to Indonesia, favouring slow-moving freshwater wetlands and marshes. Like all darters, it hunts by spearing fish underwater with a rapid lunge of its sharp bill, then surfaces to toss the prey into the air and swallow it headfirst.

children.name: Anhinga rufa
children.description: Widespread across sub-Saharan Africa and the Nile Delta, easily distinguished from cormorants by its sharply pointed bill and longer tail. A patient hunter that can remain submerged for extended periods, stalking fish among submerged vegetation.

children.name: Anhinga novaehollandiae
children.description: Occurs across Australia, New Guinea, and Indonesia, where it is often seen perched motionless with wings spread wide on a riverside snag. During the breeding season, the male develops a distinctive fine white filamentous plumage on the head and neck.
